policy_statement: W7 reduces fugitive methane and co-pollutants from WRRFs through biogas capture/use, monitoring and leak reduction, septic maintenance funding mechanism, sewer transition assistance, operator training, and research.
mechanism_description: Municipalities measure GHG emissions from WRRFs, septic, and sewer systems and reduce leaks to cut fugitive methane and related emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Wastewater/sewer/septic operations → ↓ fugitive CH₄ (± N₂O) releases via monitoring + leak repair/capture → ↓ total GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Utilities and municipalities implement emissions monitoring, inspection, and maintenance/retrofits for WRRF digesters/biogas systems, sewer networks, and septic systems to find and fix methane leakage/venting points and improve gas handling.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Reduced anaerobic-gas escape and improved containment/capture/combustion (flaring or energy use) lowers the mass of methane emitted to the atmosphere (and can also reduce process-related N2O through better operations).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower fugitive methane (and related GHG) emissions, reducing near-term warming impact and total CO2e.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing fugitive methane emissions from wastewater/sewer/septic systems, directly lowering net greenhouse gas emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Wastewater/sewer/septic system actions reduce fugitive methane emissions, producing a direct decrease in net GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While monitoring and repairs can improve operational efficiency and local air quality, the largest climate-relevant impact is the direct abatement of high-GWP methane (and sometimes N2O), which is squarely mitigation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a program emphasizes biogas use for energy, there can be an additional energy-substitution benefit, but the shared mechanism here is explicitly methane monitoring/leak reduction (direct abatement), so mitigation remains primary.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose core mechanism is measuring emissions and reducing leaks from wastewater/sewer/septic systems will principally reduce fugitive methane, i.e., mitigate climate change.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., energy recovery vs pure LDAR; inclusion of N2O controls) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a policy is framed mainly as energy efficiency/renewable energy via biogas utilization, its dominant mechanism could shift toward resource efficiency/mitigation-through-fuel-switching; however, for the stated mechanism (monitoring + leak reduction of fugitive methane), the dominant effect remains direct mitigation.
key_indicators: “need to measure”; “reduce GHG emissions”; “from WRRFs, septic, and sewer systems”; “proper equipment and training”
co_benefits: Improved local air quality (reduced methane-associated co-pollutants/odors) and operational efficiency/safety at WRRFs
instance_edge_case_notes: The excerpt does not explicitly use binding language (e.g., “shall/required”), but “will need to” implies a mandate/requirement; references to “equipment and training” could also align with technical assistance/incentives, though those instruments are not stated in the verbatim text.
